title: Quiz: Market Microstructure
description: Test your understanding of market microstructure concepts

questions.question: What is the bid-ask spread?

options.text: The difference between today's and yesterday's price
The difference between the highest bid and lowest ask price
The price movement over a day

questions.explanation: The bid-ask spread is the difference between the highest price buyers are willing to pay (bid) and the lowest price sellers are willing to accept (ask). It represents the cost of immediacy in trading.

questions.question: In price-time priority matching, which order gets filled first?

options.text: The order with the best price, or earliest if prices are equal

questions.explanation: Price-time priority means orders with better prices are matched first. Among orders at the same price, the order that arrived first has priority. This ensures fair and transparent matching.

questions.question: What is temporary market impact?

options.text: The permanent price change from trading
The price movement during execution that reverses afterward

questions.explanation: Temporary market impact is the price movement caused by the pressure of executing an order, which tends to reverse after execution completes. This is distinct from permanent impact which persists.

questions.question: What does a VWAP execution strategy aim to achieve?

options.text: Match the volume-weighted average price over the execution period
Execute only at the best price

questions.explanation: VWAP (Volume Weighted Average Price) execution aims to trade in proportion to historical volume patterns, achieving an average execution price close to the day's VWAP benchmark.

questions.question: Market makers always maintain zero inventory (no position).
questions.explanation: False. Market makers often accumulate inventory as they provide liquidity. Managing this inventory risk is a key part of market making - they adjust quotes to encourage trades that reduce unwanted positions.

questions.question: How does a market maker typically respond to building long inventory?

options.text: Widen the spread on both sides
Lower both bid and ask to encourage selling

questions.explanation: When a market maker becomes long (too much inventory), they lower both bid and ask prices. This makes their ask more attractive to buyers, encouraging them to sell inventory while reducing new buying.

questions.question: What is the square-root market impact model?

options.text: Impact is proportional to order size
Impact is proportional to the square root of order size relative to volume
Impact is proportional to the square of order size
Impact is constant regardless of size

questions.explanation: The square-root model states that price impact is proportional to the square root of order size relative to average daily volume. This means doubling order size increases impact by about 41%, not 100%.

questions.question: Which are components of total execution cost? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Total execution cost includes: crossing the bid-ask spread, temporary impact (which reverses), and permanent impact (which persists). Exchange membership fees are operational costs, not execution costs.
